toolCalls.name: apply_diff

args.diff:
# Search: |||
    status = get_ai_status()

    assert status.status == "ready"
    assert status.label == "AI Ready"
    assert status.is_available
    assert status.is_ready
    assert not status.show_enable_hint
    assert not status.is_error
    assert status.provider_info == "Claude: claude-sonnet-4-20250514"
|||
# Replace with: |||
    status = get_ai_status()

    assert status.status == "ready"
    assert status.label == "AI Ready (Claude)"
    assert status.is_available
    assert status.is_ready
    assert not status.show_enable_hint
    assert not status.is_error
    assert status.provider_info == "Claude: claude-sonnet-4-20250514"
|||

response.output: Successfully applied diff to file: /home/ovidiu/Projects/bob/proj/tests/test_assistant_state.py using MultiSearchReplace strategy. Read the file to get latest content if needed.

toolCalls.description:
Use to apply multiple PRECISE, TARGETED and SMALL modifications to one or more files by searching for specific sections of content and replacing them. apply_diff always operates on *whole* lines. the search must have exact same spacing, indentation and text. IMPORTANT: NEVER use apply_diff to replace large parts or whole file contents of a file!! use write_to_file instead.
      
      Diff format:
      ```
# Search: |||
[exact content to find including whitespace]
|||
# Start Line: [optional line number where search should begin]
# Replace with: |||
[new content to replace with]
|||
# Purpose: [optional explanation of what this replacement does]

# Search: |||
[another exact content to find]
|||
# Start Line: [optional line number]
# Replace with: |||
[replacement content]
|||
```

For multiline search or replacements, use triple |||, use another if content contains that delimiter:

```
# Search: |||
function example() {
  const x = 1;
  const y = 2;
  return x + y;
}
|||
# Start Line: 10
# Replace with: |||
function example() {
  const x = 1;
  const y = 2;
  console.log('Computing sum');
  return x + y;
}
|||
# Purpose: Add logging to the function
```

Example:

Original file:
```
1 | def calculate_total(items):
2 |     total = 0
3 |     for item in items:
4 |         total += item
5 |     return total
```

Search/Replace content:
```
# Search: |||
total += item
|||
# Start Line: 3
# Replace with: |||
total = total + item
|||
# Purpose: Make addition more explicit

# Search: |||
def calculate_total(items):
|||
# Replace with: |||
def calculate_sum(items):
|||
# Purpose: Rename function to better reflect its purpose
```

Usage:
<apply_diff>
<file_path>File path here</file_path>
<diff>
Your search/replace content here
You can use multiple search/replace blocks in one diff block
</diff>
</apply_diff>

toolCalls.description: Reads and returns the content of a specified file. If the file is large, the content will be truncated. The tool's response will clearly indicate if truncation has occurred and will provide details on how to read more of the file using the 'offset' and 'limit' parameters. Handles text, images (PNG, JPG, GIF, WEBP, SVG, BMP), and PDF files. For text files, it can read specific line ranges.

toolCalls.name: execute_command

args.description: Run full test suite to verify all tests pass after fixes

toolCalls.description:
This tool executes a given shell command as `bash -c <command>`. Command can start background processes using `&`. Command is executed as a subprocess that leads its own process group. Command process group can be terminated as `kill -- -PGID` or signaled as `kill -s SIGNAL -- -PGID`.

      The following information is returned:

      Command: Executed command.
      Directory: Directory where command was executed, or `(root)`.
      Stdout: Output on stdout stream. Can be `(empty)` or partial on error and for any unwaited background processes.
      Stderr: Output on stderr stream. Can be `(empty)` or partial on error and for any unwaited background processes.
      Error: Error or `(none)` if no error was reported for the subprocess.
      Exit Code: Exit code or `(none)` if terminated by signal.
      Signal: Signal number or `(none)` if no signal was received.
      Background PIDs: List of background processes started or `(none)`.
      Process Group PGID: Process group started or `(none)`
